rulings.Case Title: CP III RINCON TOWERS, INC., A COLORADO CORPORATION VS. ASSESSMENT APPEALS BOARD OF THE CITY ANAD COUNTY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Prevailing Parties Attorney Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 2, REAL PARTY IN INTEREST RINCON EV REALTY, LLC, A LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY's Motion For Prevailing Parties Attorney Fees The Court grants Rincon EV Realty LLC & Rincon ET Realty LLC ("Rincon")'s motion for attorneys' fees and awards $427,951.93. Rincon is a prevailing party in an action on contract with an attorney's fee provision and is entitled to attorney's fees under Civil Code section 1717 because it obtained a "simple, unqualified win" in the Court of Appeals via entry of an order denying CP III's petition. (See Hsu v. Abbara (1995) 9 Cal.4th 863, 877). Whether an action is "on a contract" is liberally construed by California courts. (Turner v. Schultz (2009) 175 Cal.App.4th 974, 979). It includes when a court is asked to "determine the parties' rights and duties under" a contract. (Excess Electronixx v. Heger Realty Corp. (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 698, 707). The Board and courts determined parties' rights arising from the Deed of Trust on which CP III relied, so CP III's petition was an action "on a contract" under Civil Code section 1717. Although CP III relied upon the Trustee's Deed Upon Sale, which does not have an attorney fee provision, the "scope and nature of the trustee's duties are exclusively defined by the deed of trust and the governing statutes." (See Kachlon v. Markowitz (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 316, 335). As in Kachlon, CP III sought a declaration of rights "arising out of" the Deed of Trust. (See Kachlon, 168 Cal.App.4th at 346-47). The Trustee's Deed Upon Sale merely carried out rights created and granted by Deed of Trust. Therefore, any request for a declaration of rights under Trustee's Deed Upon Sale necessarily involved a determination of rights provided under the Deed of Trust. (Mountain Air Enterprises, LLC v. Sundowner Towers, LLC (2017) 3 Cal.5th 744, 759 ["In determining whether a contract contains an applicable attorney fees provision, courts have "construe[d] together several documents concerning the same subject and made as part of the same transaction [citations] even though the documents were not executed contemporaneously [citation] and do not refer to each other."]). Rincon did not waive its right to attorney's fees because attorney's fees under Civil Code 1717 are treated as costs, not special damages that must be pleaded. (See Walker v. Ticor Title Co. of California (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 363, 372). Such fees are recoverable by motion. (Sears v. Baccaglio (1998) 60 Cal.App.4th 1136, 1150). Attorney's fees in the amount of $427,951.93 are reasonable because Rincon confirmed that it paid $450,475.72 in connection with administrative, trial court, and appellate court proceedings in this matter. (See PLCM Group v. Drexler (2000) 22 Cal.4th 1084, 1095 (explaining that Civil Code section 1717 provides the trial court with broad authority to determine the amount of a reasonable fee including the consideration of equitable principles). The amount requested in the Reply also falls below the lodestar of $438,435.45 based on reasonable rates and hours worked. (See Serrano v. Priest (1977) 20 Cal.3d 25, 48 n.23). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant Rincon must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ac

rulings.Case Title: SUPERISI, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION VS. ALI YALDIZ
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Arbitration And Appoint And Arbitrator
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF SUPERISI, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION's Motion To Compel Arbitration And Appoint And Arbitrator Petitioner Superisi, Inc.'s petition to compel arbitration and appoint an arbitrator is granted. The parties have entered into a valid written Dissolution and Separation Agreement and have agreed to arbitrate any controversy arising out of or related to this agreement. The parties' dispute as to the respondent's performance of his obligations under the agreement, as well as to the custody and possession of documents allegedly necessary to fulfill obligations under the agreement, are within the scope of the parties' agreement to arbitrate and should be arbitrated. (Garcia v. Superior Court (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 1138, 1144.) Pursuant to the parties' stipulation from June 5, 2018, the court appoints Hon. Ken Kawaichi (Ret.) as the arbitrator. Petitioner's request for sanctions is denied without prejudice for failing to comply with the procedural requirements of Code of Civ. Proc. § 128.5(f)(1)(A). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Petitioner Superisi, Inc. must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ac

rulings.Case Title: JOHN DAVID LESHY ET AL VS. SEAN WHELAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Defendant Sf Garage Company, Inc. To Provide Further Answers To First Set Of Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFFS JOHN LESHY, MARGARET KARP, DANIEL CHOI, CATHLEEN CABANSAG, ROY GRANT, JAMES MULLOY, JOANNE MULLOY, MARLIDA CHUNG, JAMES FULTON, KRISTIN FULTON, CAROLYN KEENAN'S Motion To Compel Defendant Sf Garage Company, Inc. To Provide Further Answers To First Set Of Interrogatories. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's unopposed motion to compel further responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Form Interrogatories to Defendant SF Garage Company, Inc. is granted. Defendant SF Garage did not provide a verification of its responses; accordingly, all objections to the interrogatories, including objections based on privilege or on the protection for work product, are waived (subject to the provisions of CCP 2030.290(a) providing for relief from such waiver). In addition, Defendant SF Garage has not provided adequate responses to Form Interrogatory 17.1, in that is has failed to identify facts on which it bases its denial of Requests for Admissions and identify supporting documents and knowledgeable witnesses. Defendant SF Garage shall provide amended responses within 14 days of notice of entry of this order. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFFS JOHN LESHY, MARGARET KARP, DANIEL CHOI, CATHLEEN CABANSAG, ROY GRANT, JAMES MULLOY, JOANNE MULLOY, MARLIDA CHUNG, JAMES FULTON, KRISTIN FULTON, CAROLYN KEENAN'S Motion To Compel Sf Garage Company's Production Of Documents. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff's unopposed motion to compel a further responses to Plaintiff's First Set of Requests for Production to Defendant SF Garage Company, Inc., and to compel further production of documents, is granted. Defendant SF Garage did not provide a verification of its responses; accordingly, all objections to the Requests for Production, including objections based on privilege or on the protection for work product, are waived (subject to the provisions of CCP 2031.300(a) providing for relief from such waiver). In addition, it appears that Defendant SF Garage has not produced documents in conformance with the statements of compliance in it (unverified) responses. Defendant SF Garage shall, within 14 days of notice of entry of this order, provide verified responses, and produce documents in conformance with its statements of compliance. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ROBERT ROBINSON ET AL VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Jordan Medina'S Motion To Challenge Confidentiality Designation Of Documents Subject To Stipulated Non Sharing Protective Order * Redacted *
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 2, Plaintiff Jordan Medina's Motion To Challenge Confidentiality Designation Of Documents Subject To Stipulated Non Sharing Protective Order * Redacted * Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Medina's motion challenging Defendant Uber's confidentiality designations pursuant to the parties' Stipulated Non-Sharing Protective Order ("SPO") is granted. Defendant has not shown that the materials in question (identified as UBER 00014-00085, UBER 00086-00090, UBER 00091-00094, UBER 00095-00198 and UBER 00208-00228) constitute or reveal trade secrets, commercially sensitive information, private information of the defendants or any third parties, or other information entitled to confidential treatment under applicable law, or that there is an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record in this proceeding. Accordingly, the materials do not qualify for designation as "Protected Material" under the SPO; Defendant is to produce copies of the materials from which the designation has been removed within 14 days of entry of this order. If any of the materials have previously been filed under seal, Plaintiff should include in the proposed order appropriate language to have them unsealed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: TIEMING YAO VS. YANYING LIU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 6, DEFENDANT YANYING LIU, QINGLING XU's Motion To Dismiss Or Stay Action The Court grants Defendants Yanying Liu and Qingling Xu's motion to stay action. The parties' loan confirmation agreements include a mandatory forum selection clause that states that disputes shall be resolved in China. (Amended Complaint, Exs. I & J.) "The California Supreme Court has held that contractual forum selection clauses are valid and should be given effect unless enforcement of the clause would be unreasonable." (Intershop Communications v. Superior Court (2002) 104 Cal.App.4th 191, 196.) Plaintiff Tieming Yao fails to show that enforcing the forum selection clause would be unreasonable. The evidence demonstrates that basically everything associated with the underlying loan transaction occurred in China. (Liu Dec., par. 4) The court rejects plaintiff's argument that the "Equity Transfer Agreement" constitutes a novation or accord that supersedes the loan agreements. (Amended Complaint, Ex. K.) There is nothing on the face of the Equity Transfer Agreement that supports plaintiff's contention. Plaintiff failed to present any evidence that the parties intended to extinguish the underlying loan obligations and replace those obligations with the Equity Transfer Agreement. (See Alexander v. Angel, (1951) 37 Cal.2d 856, 862; Carpenter v. Pacific States S. & L. Co. (1937) 19 Cal.App.2d 263, 268.) The court stays this action because Ms. Xu is not a party to the forum selection clause. Once the Chinese court resolves the dispute between Mr. Yao and Mr. Liu, Mr. Yao can return to this court to move to lift the stay and seek any other appropriate remedies. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants Yanying Liu and Qingling Xu must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ac

rulings.Case Title: JON B. EISENBERG ET AL VS. DAVE JONES ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs And Petitioners Jon B. Eisenberg And Jeff Sengstack'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, September 19, 2018, Line 10, Plaintiffs And Petitioners Jon B. Eisenberg And Jeff Sengstack'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction Plaintiffs John Eisenberg and Jeff Sengstack's motion for preliminary injunction is denied. Plaintiffs seek to compel defendants to perform their alleged mandatory duties under the law. In Common Cause v. Board of Supervisors (1989) 49 Cal.3d 432, 442, the court explains that where a public official is not performing a mandatory legal duty, the plaintiff's remedy is to pursue a writ of mandate. Here, plaintiffs do not meaningfully distinguish Common Cause; thus, they must first pursue their legal remedies. In addition, plaintiffs have not met their heavy burden to obtain a mandatory preliminary injunction against defendant. (Davenport v. Blue Cross of California (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 435, 446; Tahoe Keys Prop. Owners Ass'n v. State Water Resources Control Bd. (1994) 23 Cal.App.4th 1459, 1471.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4 p.m. stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Defendant Commissioner of the California Department of Insurance must prepare the proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ac
